title: fireground_structural_collapse
description: During an interior attack on a commercial structure fire, warning signs of structural compromise emerge. The incident commander must manage crew safety, a PAR, and a defensive transition before a partial collapse occurs.

timeline.title: Interior Attack Underway
timeline.description: Two attack crews operating inside a large commercial warehouse. Fire has been burning for approximately 35 minutes. Building is steel frame with a lightweight steel roof.

timeline.title: Structural Warning Signs
timeline.description: Crew members report cracking sounds from the roof structure. IC observes exterior wall separation on the Delta side.

timeline.title: Collapse Indicators Escalate
timeline.description: Thermal imaging shows the main roof trusses are glowing. A section of the roof sags visibly from the exterior.

timeline.title: Emergency Withdrawal
timeline.description: IC orders immediate evacuation. All crew exit. Emergency tones activated.

timeline.title: Partial Roof Collapse
timeline.description: A section of the roof collapses into the warehouse 47 seconds after the last crew member exits. No injuries.

timeline.title: Defensive Operations
timeline.description: Fire attack transitions to defensive mode. Master streams deployed from collapse zone perimeter. All crew accounted for.

node-1.title: Structural Warning Signs During Interior Attack
node-1.situation: You are the incident commander at a working commercial warehouse fire. Two attack crews have been operating inside for 35 minutes. The structure is steel frame with lightweight steel bar-joist roof construction — known to fail rapidly under fire load. You observe that the Delta-side exterior wall has a visible crack running from the roofline to mid-height. From the radio, one interior crew reports they can hear creaking from above. A Division supervisor confirms the sound is 'popping and cracking' from the roof structure.
node-1.question: What is your immediate response to these structural warning signs?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately transmit an emergency evacuation order to all interior crews. Sound the evacuation signal (three blasts on the air horn). Begin a PAR. Establish a collapse zone of one and a half times the height of the building on all exposed sides. No crew re-enters until structural assessment is complete.

options.label: B
options.text: Radio the crew to monitor the sounds and advise if conditions change. The fire attack is progressing and early withdrawal is premature.

options.label: C
options.text: Withdraw only the crew near the Delta side — the other crew can continue the attack on the Alpha side where conditions are better.

node-2.title: Crew Accountability During Evacuation
node-2.situation: The evacuation order has been transmitted. Attack Crew 1 reports they are exiting via the Alpha-side entry. Crew 2 has not responded to the radio. The air horn is sounding. Bystanders report that Crew 2 was last seen advancing toward the rear of the warehouse. You have a RIT team standing by. The roof cracking sounds are audible from the exterior. You have no confirmation that all crew members are out.
node-2.question: How do you account for Crew 2?

options.label: A
options.text: Transmit repeated radio calls to Crew 2 on the primary channel and the emergency channel simultaneously. Activate the RIT and position them at the nearest entry point — do not yet commit them inside without confirmation of location. Request a visual sweep by any exterior crew near the rear of the structure.

options.label: B
options.text: Deploy RIT immediately inside the warehouse to find Crew 2 — speed is the priority.

options.label: C
options.text: Assume Crew 2 is exiting and complete the PAR when they emerge — do not commit additional resources until confirmed missing.

node-3.title: Crew 2 Responds
node-3.situation: Crew 2 transmits: 'We copy evacuation — we're at the rear of the building near the loading dock. We are advancing toward the rear exit now.' Simultaneously, a collapse groan is audible from the roof structure and the Delta-side wall moves visibly outward. You estimate the roof could fail within 60 seconds. The rear exit (loading dock) is on the Delta-Charlie corner — potentially within the collapse zone.
node-3.question: How do you direct Crew 2's exit?

options.label: A
options.text: Radio Crew 2 immediately: 'Do not exit via the loading dock — Delta side is collapsing. Exit via the Alpha-side hose line — follow your hose out the front. Move now.' Confirm they acknowledge. Position a crew member with a light at the Alpha entry to guide them.

options.label: B
options.text: Tell Crew 2 to exit via the nearest door — the loading dock is closer and speed is the priority.

options.label: C
options.text: Tell Crew 2 to shelter in place at the rear — the collapse will be limited to the Delta side and sheltering is safer than moving through the fire.

node-4.title: Partial Collapse and PAR
node-4.situation: Crew 2 exits via the Alpha-side entry 45 seconds after your radio instruction. At that moment, a section of the roof — approximately 20 metres of bar-joist construction — collapses into the building near the loading dock. The collapse is audible across the fireground. All interior crews are now accounted for — Crew 1 and Crew 2 are both exterior. No injuries reported. The fire is now venting through the collapse hole. Your attack lines are abandoned inside. You must now transition to defensive operations.
node-4.question: How do you manage the transition to defensive attack?

options.label: A
options.text: Confirm full PAR with all crews and report to dispatch. Establish and enforce the collapse zone perimeter — remove all personnel to beyond one and a half times the building height. Deploy ground monitors and tower ladders from outside the collapse zone. Do not re-enter. Begin a defensive master stream attack.

options.label: B
options.text: Re-enter the building with a crew to retrieve the abandoned hose lines — equipment loss is significant.

options.label: C
options.text: Position apparatus at the Alpha side for a close-in defensive attack — the collapse was on the Delta side and other sides may be structurally sound.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: All crew members were evacuated safely before the structural collapse. Your rapid recognition of multi-indicator collapse signs — wall separation, audible cracking, sagging roof — triggered a timely evacuation order. You correctly redirected Crew 2 away from the collapse zone exit, maintained accountability throughout, and executed a clean transition to defensive operations with a properly established collapse zone. No injuries occurred.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
